body_txt: The distant, somewhat more maligned cousin of the Preppy Name and Hayseed Name . Over the last roughly 40 years of the USA's history, a trend has emerged among the country's working poor — most notably among African Americans — to give their children unique, highly distinctive names. Many of these names are oddly and perhaps intentionally misspelled , or pronounced unusually , and often somewhat grandiose. The media finds this trend endlessly amusing, and names like this are now firmly tied to stereotypes of poverty. It must be noted that not all of these names are just "made up out of nowhere". Many of these so-called ghetto names have a French/Creole origin or inspiration (using prefixes such as Le', De', Fa'), or are borrowed from languages commonly spoken in Africa, like Arabic (Latifah, Hakeem, Jamal) or Swahili (Jabari, Imani). Regardless, because of their unfamiliarity, these names are subject to considerable stigma and mockery in American media. If you see anyone with a name like "Sha'quayla Joniqua LeBrontayyy " appear in a piece of media, she's almost certainly meant to be a target of derision — quite probably a Fat Idiot Sassy Black Woman with multiple kids who serves as an attempt at Plucky Comic Relief — and sometimes not even that . The Unfortunate Implications of this are not hard to see. Spanish-speaking countries, LatAm in particular, have their counterpart with English-originated names. It's become a meme that if you're named Bryan/Kevin or Kimberly/Brittany, or name your kids Bryan/Kevin or Kimberly/Brittany, it's indicative of poverty and identification with the cholo/narco subculture. It's seen as worse if the name has been españolizado (Spanish-ized). A possible Sub-Trope of Who Names Their Kid "Dude"? , Unfortunate Names , and Unfortunate Implications if the name is outlandish enough. noreallife
